title: Designing Component Trees

learningObjectives.text: Explain splitting a ui into components using a new example and justify the result.
Explain component boundaries using a new example and justify the result.
Explain component categories using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: Designing Component Trees is part of the mental model learners need before making reliable implementation decisions. It belongs to the **Component Design and Reusability** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Splitting a UI into components** → **Component boundaries** → **Component categories**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Splitting a UI into components
sections.body: Split where a region has a coherent responsibility, repeated pattern, independent state, or meaningful data boundary.

sections.title: Component boundaries
sections.body: A component boundary defines ownership of markup, behavior, and dependencies. Good boundaries minimize reasons to change.

sections.title: Component categories
sections.body: Page, layout, feature, and reusable UI categories can clarify ownership and dependency direction, but they are team conventions rather than React rules.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: The page is split along distinct responsibilities.

commonMistakes: Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
Using global state before identifying the real owner and consumers.
Optimizing renders without a measured user-facing problem.
summary: Designing Component Trees connects Splitting a UI into components, Component boundaries, Component categories.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “Splitting a UI into components” in the context of Designing Component Trees?

choices.label: Split where a region has a coherent responsibility, repeated pattern, independent state, or meaningful data boundary.
choices.feedback: Splitting a UI into components is best understood as follows: Split where a region has a coherent responsibility, repeated pattern, independent state, or meaningful data boundary.

choices.label: Keys give siblings stable identity across insertion, removal, and reordering. Use data identifiers rather than indexes for changing lists.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Splitting a UI into components.

choices.label: Evaluate alternatives by complexity, clarity, testability, performance, team understanding, and likely change rather than searching for a universally perfect pattern.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Splitting a UI into components.

choices.label: Do not use Effects for render-time derivation, click logic, or duplicate-state synchronization.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Splitting a UI into components.

questions.explanation: Splitting a UI into components is best understood as follows: Split where a region has a coherent responsibility, repeated pattern, independent state, or meaningful data boundary.
questions.hint: Focus on what the concept changes, enables, or constrains—not only on its syntax.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Designing Component Trees in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Apply Splitting a UI into components deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Designing Component Trees.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Designing Component Trees.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Designing Component Trees.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: Which response best addresses this common mistake: “Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.”?

choices.label: The statement is unrelated to program behavior and cannot cause defects.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Designing Component Trees.

choices.label: The statement is always correct, so no further reasoning about Designing Component Trees is needed.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Designing Component Trees.

choices.label: The statement is a misconception. A better approach is to reason from the concept's inputs, rules, and observable outcome, then verify the result.
choices.feedback: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.

choices.label: The statement is correct only when variable names are short.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Designing Component Trees.

questions.explanation: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.
questions.hint: Look for the option that corrects both the belief and the learner's next action.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Designing Component Trees?

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Splitting a UI into components, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Designing Component Trees.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Designing Component Trees.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Designing Component Trees.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Designing Component Trees?

choices.label: Avoid Designing Component Trees completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Designing Component Trees.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Designing Component Trees.

choices.label: Use Designing Component Trees in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Designing Component Trees.

choices.label: Use Designing Component Trees when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
